concepts.title: Definition and example of range
concepts.conceptBody:
Range 

For a given function $\text{y}=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$, the set of values which $\text{y}$ can take, corresponding to each real number in the domain, is known as Range of function. for ex. $y={{x}^{2}}$, here $\text{x}$ can take all real values but $\text{y}$ can take only positive values. Domain & Range can also be expressed as

concepts.title: Domain and Range of Trigonometric functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Trigonometry/circular functions 

Functions involving trigonometric ratios are called trigonometric functions.

(a) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{sin}x$ 

Interval in which the inverse can be obtained : $\left[ -\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right]$

(b) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{cos}x$ 

Interval in which the inverse can be obtained : $\left[ 0,\pi \right]$

(c) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{tan}x$ 

Interval in which the inverse can be obtained : $\left( -\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right)$

(d) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{cot}x$ 

Interval in which the inverse can be obtained $:\left( 0,\pi \right)$

(e) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{sec}x$ 

Interval in which the inverse can be obtained : $\left[ 0,\frac{\pi }{2} \right)\cup \left( \frac{\pi }{2},\pi \right]$

(f) $y=f\left( x \right)=\text{cosec}x$ 

Interval in which the inverse can be obtained : $\left[ -\frac{\pi }{2},0 \right)\cup \left( 0,\frac{\pi }{2} \right]$

concepts.title: Steps to be adopted for finding Range of any general function
concepts.conceptBody:
Range

Working Rule: 

First of all, find the domain.

  1. If domain does not contain an interval, find the value of $x$ putting the values of $x$ from the domain. The set of all these values of $y$ will be the range.
  2. If function is continuous and domain contains only finite intervals, find the least and greatest values of $\text{y}$ for values of $\text{x}$ in the domain. If $\alpha $ and $\beta $ be the least and greatest values of $\text{y}$ for values of $x$ in the domain, then range $f=\left[ \alpha ,\beta \right]$. In order to find the least and greatest values of $y$, write down the sign scheme for $\frac{dy}{dx}$.

This method of finding the range of $f\left( x \right)$ can also be used when domain is $R$ or contains an infinite interval provided $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is continuous in the domain.

If domain is $\text{R}$ or the set of all real numbers except a few points, then express $\text{x}$ in terms of $\text{y}$ and from this, find the value of $\text{y}$ for which $\text{x}$ is real and belongs to the domain. The set of all these values of $y$ will be the range. But if domain does not contain some points say $\alpha $ and $\beta $, then find $\text{y}$ when $\text{x}=\alpha $, and $\text{x}=\beta $ and exclude these values of $\text{y}$

Q) Find domain and range of the function $y=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{e}}\left( 3{{x}^{2}}-4x+5 \right)$. 

Solution : 

$\text{y}$ is defined if $3{{\text{x}}^{2}}-4\text{x}+5>0$

Where $\text{D}=16-4\left( 3 \right)\left( 5 \right)=-44<0$

and coefficient of ${{\text{x}}^{2}}=3>0$

Hence, $\left( 3{{\text{x}}^{2}}-4\text{x}+5 \right)>0\forall \text{x}\in \text{R}$

Thus, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Domain }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\in \text{R}$

Now, $\text{y}=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\left( 3{{\text{x}}^{2}}-4\text{x}+5 \right)$

We have $3{{\text{x}}^{2}}-4\text{x}+5={{\text{e}}^{\text{y}}}$

Or $3{{\text{x}}^{2}}-4\text{x}+\left( 5-{{\text{e}}^{\text{y}}} \right)=0$

Since, $\text{x}$ is real thus, discriminate $\ge 0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }12{{\text{e}}^{\text{y}}}\ge 44$

So, $\text{y}\ge \text{log}\left( \frac{11}{3} \right)$

Hence, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ range }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \text{log}\left( \frac{11}{3} \right),\infty \right)$

Q) Find the range of the function $f\left( x \right)=\left[ \text{ln}\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+x+1} \right) \right]$, where [.] denotes the 

greatest integer function. 

Solution : 

Given, $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\left[ \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1} \right) \right]$ ……..(1)

Domain of $f$ : 

For $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ to be defined, 

(i) ${{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1\ge 0$ $\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-\infty <\text{x}<\infty $ …….(A)

(ii) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-1\le \sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1}\le 1$ $\Rightarrow \,\,\,{{x}^{2}}+x+1\le 1\left[ \because {{x}^{2}}+x+1>0 \right]$

$\begin{align} & \Rightarrow \,\,\,{{x}^{2}}+x+1\le 1\,\Rightarrow \,{{x}^{2}}+x\,\le \,0 \\ & \Rightarrow \,\,\,-1\,\le \,x\,\le \,0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,........(B) \\ \end{align}$

(iii) $\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1}>0$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1}>\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1}>0\left[ \therefore \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ an }\!\!~\!\!\text{ increasing }\!\!~\!\!\text{ function }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right] \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1>0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-\infty <\text{x}<\infty \\ \end{array}$

From (A), (B) and (C), $-1\le \text{x}\le 0$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Domain $\text{f}=\left[ -1,0 \right]$

Range of $f$ : 

Least value of ${{x}^{2}}+x+1={{\left( x+\frac{1}{2} \right)}^{2}}+\frac{3}{4}=\frac{3}{4}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ at $x=\frac{-1}{2}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ least value of $\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

Also $\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1}\le 1,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ {} \right.$ Since $\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1}$ should be defined $]$

Thus $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\le \sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1}\le 1$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)\le \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+x+1}\le \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( 1 \right)$

$\left[ \therefore \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x} \right.$ is an increasing function]

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\pi }{3}\le \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1}\le \frac{\pi }{2}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\left( \frac{\pi }{3} \right)\le \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1} \right)\le \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\left( \frac{\pi }{2} \right)$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }0<\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1} \right)<1\left[ \because \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\left( \frac{\pi }{2} \right)<1 \right]\And \left[ \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\frac{\pi }{3}>0 \right]$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\text{x}+1} \right) \right]=0$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)=0$ for all $\text{x}\in \left[ -1,0 \right]$

Hence range $\text{f}=\left\{ 0 \right\}$

Note : Here $f$ is a many-one function.

Q) Find the range of $f\left( x \right)=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{e}}\frac{1}{\left[ \text{cos}x \right]-\left[ \text{sin}x \right]}$ where $\left[ x \right]$ denotes the integral part of $x$. 

Solution : 

Given, $f\left( x \right)=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{e}}\frac{1}{\left[ \text{cos}x \right]-\left[ \text{sin}x \right]}$

 

Domain of $f$ : 

For $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ to be defined,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \left[ \text{cosx} \right]-\left[ \text{sinx} \right]>0 \\ \end{array}$

$\begin{align} & \Rightarrow \,\,[\cos \,x]>\,[\sin \,x] \\ & \Rightarrow \,\,-\frac{\pi }{2}\,\le \,x\,\le \,0 \\ & Domain\,\,=\,\,[-\frac{\pi }{2},0] \\ \end{align}$

Range of $\text{f}:\text{In}\left[ -\frac{\pi }{2},0 \right],\left[ \text{cosx} \right]-\left[ \text{sinx} \right]=1$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ from (1), $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{e}}}1=0$

Hence range $f=\left\{ 0 \right\}$

Note : Here $f$ is a many-one function.

Q) Find the range of the following functions : 
(i) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }f\left( x \right)=\text{ln}\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+4x+5}$ (ii) $f\left( x \right)=3\text{sin}x+8\text{cos}\left( x-\frac{\pi }{3} \right)+5$ 
(iii) $f\left( x \right)=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left[ \frac{1}{2}+{{x}^{2}} \right]$ (iv) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\frac{2\text{x}-2}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}-2\text{x}+3}$ 

([.] denotes the greatest integer function) 

Solution : 

(i) $\text{ Here}f\left( x \right)=\text{ln}\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+4x+5}=\text{ln}\sqrt{{{(x+2)}^{2}}+1}$

i.e. ${{x}^{2}}+4x+5$ takes all values in $\left[ 1,\infty \right)\Rightarrow f\left( x \right)$ will take all values in $\left[ 0,\infty \right)$.

Hence range of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is $\left[ 0,\infty \right)$.

(ii) $$ Here $f\left( x \right)=3\text{sin}x+8\text{cos}\left( x-\frac{\pi }{3} \right)+5$

$=3\text{sin}x+4\left( \text{cos}x+\sqrt{3}\text{sin}x \right)+5=\left( 3+4\sqrt{3} \right)\text{sin}x+\text{cos}x+5$.

Put $3+4\sqrt{3}=r\text{cos}\theta $ and $4=r\text{sin}\theta $ so that

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \text{r}=\sqrt{73+24\sqrt{3}}\text{ and }\theta =\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{4}{3+4\sqrt{3}}\Rightarrow \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\sqrt{73+24\sqrt{3}}\text{sin}\left( \text{x}+\theta \right)+5 \\ {} & \Rightarrow \text{ Range }\text{ of }\text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{ is }\left[ 5-\sqrt{73+24\sqrt{3}},5+\sqrt{73+24\sqrt{3}} \right] \\ \end{array}$

(iii) Here $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left[ \frac{1}{2}+{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right]$

For any value of $x,\left[ \frac{1}{2}+{{x}^{2}} \right]$ is a non-negative integer and $\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}$ is defined only for

two non-negative integers 0 and 1 .

$\Rightarrow $ the range of $\text{f}=\left\{ 0,\frac{\pi }{2} \right\}$

(iv) Here $f\left( x \right)=\frac{2x-2}{{{x}^{2}}-2x+3}$

Let $\text{y}=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ i.e. $\text{y}=\frac{2\text{x}-2}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}-2\text{x}+3}\Rightarrow \text{y}{{\text{x}}^{2}}-2\left( \text{y}+1 \right)\text{x}+3\text{y}+2=0$

which is a quadratic in $\text{x}$. For above quadratic to have real roots $\Delta\ge 0$

$\Rightarrow 4{{(\text{y}+1)}^{2}}-4\text{y}\left( 3\text{y}+2 \right)\ge 0$

$\Rightarrow {{y}^{2}}\le \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\text{ y}\le \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

Hence the range of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is $\left[ -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \right]$.
